headlines.geopolitics: Iranian Military Shows It Knows How to Adapt, U.S. Officials Say
headlines.economy: Culture at Columbia Shielded Sexual Assault by Physician, Report Finds
headlines.domestic: Fox News Poll: Voters expect AI to transform our lives — but today is not that day

metadata.daily_do: Check your own AI detection skills — try identifying if something is AI-generated before checking. Building that muscle now matters more than waiting for perfect tools.

clips.caption: When Duke realizes he might be using AI without knowing it 😅
Dr. Pringle drops historical truth about technology adoption
Jax's take on aliens vs AI priorities has us dying

script.text: Wait, so you're telling me that thing I've been using to write thank you notes might actually be— oh man, Jenny's gonna kill me.
It's Monday, March 10th. I'm Leo Barros, and this is The Friction. Let's start with what we know.

Geopolitics first. U.S. officials are reporting that Iran's military is showing tactical adaptability, specifically targeting what they see as American vulnerabilities in our regional air defense systems.
Of course they are.
The Times is reporting this as Iran probing for weaknesses in systems designed to protect U.S. troops and assets in the region.
How is this different from what they've been doing for, like, the past decade?

Good question. We'll get into that. Economy next — an external investigation found that Columbia University and NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital mishandled sexual assault allegations against physician Robert Hadden.
Two affiliated doctors have resigned following the report's findings about what investigators called a culture that enabled the abuse.
Institutional protection over individual accountability. Tale as old as time, I'm afraid.
Wait, is this the same Columbia that's been in the news for like seventeen other scandals this year?

Different issue, same institution. And domestically, a Fox News poll shows eighty-seven percent of voters expect AI to transform American life, but only forty-seven percent find it useful in their daily activities right now.
So we think it's coming but we're not there yet. That actually makes sense.
Eighty-nine percent want disclosure requirements for AI content. I mean, yeah, obviously.
Also, seventy-four percent of people think aliens exist. Just throwing that out there.
We'll get to the aliens, Jax. Let's start with Iran.
So, Duke, you served in the region. When officials say Iran is showing 'tactical adaptability,' what does that actually mean?
It means they're not stupid. Look, every military studies their opponent's weaknesses. The fact that we're surprised by this is kind of embarrassing.
But isn't the bigger question why we have air defense vulnerabilities in the first place? I mean, we spend more on defense than the next ten countries combined.
Because perfect defense doesn't exist, Bree. Every system has gaps. The question is whether you adapt faster than the other guy finds them.
The Times suggests Iran is specifically targeting what they perceive as American vulnerabilities. Duke, is this escalation or just intelligence gathering?
Probably both. They probe, we respond, they learn from our response. It's like— Jenny and I do this dance when we're fighting. She pokes, I react, she learns what buttons to push next time.
Did Duke just compare Iranian military strategy to marriage counseling?
I'm saying conflict patterns are conflict patterns, smart guy.
But here's what I don't get. If we know they're probing for weaknesses, and we know where our vulnerabilities are, why not just— I don't know, fix them?
Because you fix one, three more pop up. It's not whack-a-mole, it's chess. Every move creates new positions.
Bree, you've been critical of our Middle East presence. Does this change your calculus?

Look, I still think we're overextended. But if Iran is actively testing our defenses, pulling back right now sends the wrong signal. It's like— okay, bad analogy time — it's like leaving your house unlocked because you're tired of buying better locks.
That's actually not a terrible analogy.
Thank you. I think.
So what's the play here? More defense spending? Diplomatic engagement? Both?
You shore up the defenses first. Then you talk. Diplomacy from weakness is just begging.
But Duke, that's exactly the thinking that got us into this arms race in the first place. At some point, someone has to—
Someone has to what? Trust Iran? Come on.
Not trust. Engage. There's a difference between trusting someone and talking to them.
Can I just say, as the guy who doesn't know anything about military strategy, this whole conversation is giving me anxiety about things I didn't know I should be anxious about?
Welcome to foreign policy, Jax. Professor, you've been quiet. Historical perspective?
Oh, I'm saving the good stuff for my segment. But I will say this— every empire thinks it's the first to face adaptive adversaries. None of them are.
Alright, Professor, the floor is yours.
Thank you, Leo. Now, when I hear about Iran probing American defenses, I'm reminded of something that happened in October 1962.
The Soviets had been testing American radar coverage over Alaska for months. Not with missiles— with aircraft. Flying patterns, turning back, seeing how we responded.
Wait, during the Cuban Missile Crisis?
Precisely. While Kennedy and Khrushchev were playing nuclear chess in the Caribbean, Soviet pilots were mapping our blind spots in the Arctic. Classic misdirection.
So you're saying Iran might be doing this while we're focused somewhere else?
I'm saying adversaries always probe when they think you're distracted. The Soviets knew we were fixated on Cuba. What's Iran betting we're fixated on now?
Ukraine? China? Our own political chaos?
Could be any of those. But here's the thing about 1962— those Soviet test flights actually helped us. We discovered gaps in our coverage and fixed them before they mattered.
So Iran testing our defenses might actually be doing us a favor?
If we're smart about it. The Soviets taught us where we were vulnerable. The question is whether we learn the lesson or just get angry about the test.
Huh. So maybe we should be thanking them?
I wouldn't go that far. But historically, the militaries that adapt to probing become stronger. The ones that just complain about it get surprised later.
This is why I love history class with you, Doc. It's like therapy but with more nuclear weapons.
That might be the most accurate description of my field I've ever heard.
Alright, let's shift gears. Jax, what caught your eye today?
Oh man, where do I start? First up, we got this AI poll that's basically Americans saying 'We think robots are coming for us but they're not here yet so pass the popcorn.'
I mean, that's not wrong though. Eighty-seven percent think AI will change everything, but only forty-seven percent find it useful now.
Right? It's like when everyone said the internet would change everything in 1995, but half the websites still looked like they were designed by someone's nephew.
Hold on. Forty-seven percent find it useful? What are people using AI for?
Writing emails, apparently. And like, homework help. Oh, and Duke, you're gonna love this— the poll shows men under forty-five are most likely to find it useful.
I'm forty-three.
And you're a Republican, which makes you even more likely to find it useful according to this thing.
I don't use AI.
Duke, do you use Google Maps?
Yeah, obviously.
That's AI. Do you use predictive text when you're texting Jenny?
Well, yeah, but that's just—
AI. Do you have Netflix recommendations?
Wait, so you're telling me that thing I've been using to write thank you notes might actually be— oh man, Jenny's gonna kill me.
What do you mean write thank you notes?
My phone has this thing where it suggests what to say when I'm texting people thank you messages. I thought it was just being helpful.
Duke, you've been using AI to write thank you notes?
I guess? Jenny always says my messages sound more thoughtful lately. She's gonna think I'm a fraud.
This is exactly what the poll is talking about. Sixty percent of people think they can tell if something is AI-generated, but—
But Duke's been accidentally catfishing his own wife with robot thank you notes.
That's not— okay, that's probably exactly what I've been doing.
You know, this reminds me of when spell check first became common. People worried it would make us all terrible spellers.
Did it?
I can't tell. None of my students spell anything correctly anymore, but they also don't seem to care.
But here's what's interesting about the poll— eighty-nine percent want disclosure requirements. People want to know when they're interacting with AI.
Yeah, well, apparently I needed a disclosure requirement for my own phone.
The best part of this poll is that eighty percent of people are worried AI is eroding trust in news and social media. Like, have you seen social media? Trust was already dead and buried.
Fair point. What else caught your eye?
Oh, the alien thing. Seventy-four percent of Americans think life exists or existed on other planets. Which is actually the most rational thing in this entire poll.
I mean, statistically, given the size of the universe—
Right? But here's what kills me— we're worried about AI taking over humanity, but three-quarters of us think aliens probably exist. Like, what's our priority list here?
At least aliens haven't been writing my thank you notes without me knowing about it.
That you know of.

You know, there's actually a fascinating historical parallel to this AI adoption pattern. In the 1990s, everyone predicted the internet would revolutionize commerce, but most people were still afraid to enter their credit card numbers online.
And now we buy everything online without thinking about it.
Exactly. The gap between expectation and adoption is usually about comfort and trust. People need time to figure out what's safe.
Speaking of trust, let's talk about Columbia University for a minute.
Oh right, the sexual assault cover-up report. Man, Columbia is having a year.
This is about institutional culture, right? Like, it's not just one bad actor, it's the system that protected him.
The external report found that administrators mishandled allegations against physician Robert Hadden. Two affiliated doctors resigned after the findings.
How do you mishandle something like that? Either you investigate properly or you don't.

Oh, institutions are remarkably creative when it comes to protecting their reputation over individual safety. We saw it at Penn State, we've seen it in the Catholic Church, we see it everywhere powerful institutions feel threatened.
The pattern is always the same. Minimize, isolate, hope it goes away. Until it doesn't.
And then they act shocked when an external investigation finds exactly what everyone suspected. It's like, guys, we could have told you this was gonna happen.
The question is whether resignations actually fix the culture or just remove the visible problem.
Probably depends on who replaces them and what incentives they face.
Right. If the institution doesn't change its decision-making process, you just get new people making the same mistakes.
Professor, you mentioned this pattern before. Is there a historical lesson here?
Oh, absolutely. You know, when the printing press was invented in the 1440s, the Catholic Church's initial response was to try to control who could own one.
They were terrified that if anyone could print books, people might start reading things they weren't supposed to read. Dangerous ideas might spread.
Wait, are we talking about AI or the printing press?

Both. Every transformative technology goes through the same cycle. First, institutions try to control it. Then they try to ignore it. Then they try to adapt to it. Finally, they pretend they invented it.
So where are we with AI?
Somewhere between trying to control it and trying to ignore it. The fact that eighty-nine percent want disclosure requirements? That's the control phase.
But is that wrong? I mean, shouldn't people know when they're interacting with AI?

Not wrong, just historically predictable. The printing press had to be licensed. Radio had to be regulated. Television had to be controlled. The internet was supposed to be civilized. How'd that work out?
So you're saying we're gonna end up with AI everywhere whether we want it or not?
I'm saying Duke's phone is already writing his thank you notes, and he didn't even know it. That's usually how these things go.
Thanks for bringing that up again, Professor.
Alright, let's wrap this up. Daily Do time.
That poll showed sixty percent of people think they can tell if something is AI-generated. But Duke's story suggests maybe we're not as good at that as we think.
So here's what you can do today. Before you check if something is AI-generated, try to guess. Build that detection muscle while the stakes are still low.
Look at an email, a social media post, even a text message suggestion. Ask yourself— does this sound human or not? Then check if you can.
The goal isn't to become paranoid about AI. It's to become literate about it. Because Professor Pringle's right— it's coming whether we're ready or not.
I'm starting with my own phone, apparently.
Duke's gonna have the most awkward conversation with Jenny tonight.
Honey, remember all those thoughtful thank you texts? Well, funny story...
At least now you know why they sounded so much better than your usual messages.
Wow. Thanks, Bree. Really helpful.
If I may leave you with a thought from Arthur C. Clarke— 'Any sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic.'
The magic isn't scary once you understand how the trick works.
And on that note, this has been The Friction. I'm Jax Mooney, reminding you that seventy-four percent of Americans believe in aliens, but only forty-seven percent find AI useful.
Make of that what you will. We'll see you tomorrow, assuming the robots haven't written our show for us by then.
Please don't give them any ideas.
Too late. My phone is already suggesting what I should say next.
